title: Today's Tech Brief: Exclusive: Office workers drive OpenAI's Codex growth - Axios
summary: A look at OpenAI's Codex growth and its implications
imageAlt: Image from Axios for Exclusive: Office workers drive OpenAI's Codex growth - Axios
imageCaption: Exclusive: Office workers drive OpenAI's Codex growth - Axios (Axios)

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and I'm here to walk you through some of the latest tech headlines. Today, I'm looking at a report from Axios about OpenAI's Codex growth. For those who might not know, Codex is an AI model developed by OpenAI that can generate code in various programming languages. It's like a super-smart coding assistant that can help developers write code faster and more efficiently.

According to Axios, office workers are driving the growth of OpenAI's Codex. It seems that non-technical employees are using Codex to automate tasks and create tools that would typically require a developer. This is interesting because it shows how AI-powered tools are becoming more accessible to a wider range of people, not just tech-savvy folks. I mean, who wouldn't want to automate some of their tedious tasks, right?

Another news item that caught my attention is the growing trend of AI-powered virtual events. With the pandemic still fresh in our minds, virtual events have become the norm. AI is being used to create more immersive and engaging experiences for attendees. For instance, AI-powered chatbots are being used to facilitate networking and match attendees with similar interests. It's like having a personal event assistant at your disposal.

Exclusive: Office workers drive OpenAI's Codex growth - Axios, from Axios, is also worth a quick mention. The main content says: Comprehensive up-to-date news coverage, aggregated from sources all over the world by Google News. Exclusive: Office workers drive OpenAI's Codex growth Axios The point is not to force it into a giant theory; it is simply part of today's news mix.

My quick read is that today's set of stories is useful because it gives us texture, not a sermon. Each item has its own little job: one tells us what changed, another tells us what people are worried about, and another gives the day its mood.
So that is the brief version: a few headlines, a little context, and just enough connective tissue to make the day easier to follow without turning the whole thing into homework.
